title: Function contracts and return values

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Function contracts and return values; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A find_user function documents that it borrows the repository, returns optional<User>, performs no mutation, and can allocate while searching. Your goal is to design and implement cohesive functions with testable contracts. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and range iteration; pf abstraction calls; pf abstraction functions; Fundamental types and initialization. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A C++ function contract states accepted values and ownership, result and error behavior, side effects, complexity where relevant, and exception guarantees. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: A type signature cannot express every semantic precondition, noexcept is a termination promise rather than an error handler, and references do not encode ownership alone. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: If parameter and return types compile, all valid domain inputs and failure behavior are already defined by the signature.

includes: Specify preconditions, outputs and effects and prefer return values for results.
Design and implement cohesive functions with testable contracts.
excludes: A type signature cannot express every semantic precondition, noexcept is a termination promise rather than an error handler, and references do not encode ownership alone.

outcomes: Design and implement cohesive functions with testable contracts.

examples.title: Function contracts and return values: defined case
examples.situation: A find_user function documents that it borrows the repository, returns optional<User>, performs no mutation, and can allocate while searching.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A find_user function documents that it borrows the repository, returns optional<User>, performs no mutation, and can allocate while searching.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Document and test semantic preconditions, ownership, effects, errors, and guarantees in addition to the C++ type signature.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Document and test semantic preconditions, ownership, effects, errors, and guarantees in addition to the C++ type signature.

examples.title: Function contracts and return values: boundary case
examples.situation: A swap operation states the no-throw guarantee needed by a container and tests that invariant-preserving behavior.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A swap operation states the no-throw guarantee needed by a container and tests that invariant-preserving behavior.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: A type signature cannot express every semantic precondition, noexcept is a termination promise rather than an error handler, and references do not encode ownership alone.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: If parameter and return types compile, all valid domain inputs and failure behavior are already defined by the signature.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and implement cohesive functions with testable contracts.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If parameter and return types compile, all valid domain inputs and failure behavior are already defined by the signature.
misconceptions.correction: Document and test semantic preconditions, ownership, effects, errors, and guarantees in addition to the C++ type signature.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: If parameter and return types compile, all valid domain inputs and failure behavior are already defined by the signature.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Function contracts and return values: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
A type signature cannot express every semantic precondition, noexcept is a termination promise rather than an error handler, and references do not encode ownership alone.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and implement cohesive functions with testable contracts. Keep this boundary in view: A type signature cannot express every semantic precondition, noexcept is a termination promise rather than an error handler, and references do not encode ownership alone.
